coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots anchored on the anti-reflection silica layer as solid luminescence down-shifting materials in solar panel encapsulation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Tiezheng
Tang, Yao
Fan, Haonan
Liu, Su
Zeng, Shengqu
Liu, Wei
coredata.dc:description: Using the ultraviolet (UV) spectrum of light to enhance the output of encapsulated solar panels will promote the wider use of solar power. In this work, fluorescent carbon quantum dots anchored on porous silica film with preferable yellow/orange emission were prepared via solvothermal fabrication, the sol-gel method, and coating onto the top glass of a solar panel in sequence. Structural evolution investigations, optical characterizations, and performance evaluations were carried out to demonstrate the anchoring effects. The film was dual-functional due to its inclusion of anti-reflection (AR) and luminescence down-shifting (LDS) materials. This solid material provided a better means of solar panel encapsulation and achieved electrical gain, as the anchoring effect avoided the dispersion of LDS materials inside the encapsulation. Placing the film on top of the solar panel could guarantee the best UV absorption/conversion for LDS. In conclusion, the inclusion of a new fluorescent material was compatible with the standard AR process of photovoltaic glass with no additional cost.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Anti-reflection silica
Luminescence
Solar panel

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ethanol-based luminescent CQD and CQD/silica sol preparation
Mini solar panel fabrication with dual-functional silica coating

This study was financially supported by the ?HengCai JiaoZhi 2020 Nr.67? with contractNr.202010031490 Hengyang science and technology Bureau, by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 2020JJ4261), by the Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department, China (Grant No. 20A143) and scientific research project of Hunan Provincial Department of Education (Grant No. 18B463).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of (a) ethanol-based CQD and (b) CQD-linked silica oligomer.

(a) X‐ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results for glass surface-anchored CQDs, (b) XPS results for prime glass, (c) C1s spectra, (d) O1s spectra, (e) N1s spectra, and (f) Si 2p spectra, indicating that N-doped CQDs were embedded into the silica matrix.

UV–Vis transmission spectra of 4 different silica glass samples: glass + prime AR coating, glass + prime AR coating+5% concentration CQD anchoring, glass + prime AR coating+7.5% concentration CQD anchoring, glass + prime AR coating+10% concentration CQD anchoring. High CQD loading may impair the total transmission; the inset shows that the glass with CQD anchoring has higher UV absorption.

The absorption spectra of CQD sample shows strong absorption around 370 nm. PL spectra of the CQDs under two different conditions: hydrolyzed TEOS solution and anchored solid silica glass; all PL spectra were obtained by 360 nm excitation.
Measured I–V plots of two mini solar panels, showing that the short-circuit current (Isc) of the panel improves by 0.09 A with CQD anchoring.
Measured EQE curves of two solar panels, showing that the EQE of the panel with AR + CQD anchoring is high in the 300–400 nm UV band, because of the high absorption of the CQDs in this band.
PV test parameters of mini solar panels under the condition of AM 1.5 illuminations.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots anchored on the anti-reflection silica layer as solid luminescence down-shifting materials in solar panel encapsulation

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Institute of Automobile Parts Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using the ultraviolet (UV) spectrum of light to enhance the output of encapsulated solar panels will promote the wider use of solar power. In this work, fluorescent carbon quantum dots anchored on porous silica film with preferable yellow/orange emission were prepared via solvothermal fabrication, the sol-gel method, and coating onto the top glass of a solar panel in sequence. Structural evolution investigations, optical characterizations, and performance evaluations were carried out to demonstrate the anchoring effects. The film was dual-functional due to its inclusion of anti-reflection (AR) and luminescence down-shifting (LDS) materials. This solid material provided a better means of solar panel encapsulation and achieved electrical gain, as the anchoring effect avoided the dispersion of LDS materials inside the encapsulation. Placing the film on top of the solar panel could guarantee the best UV absorption/conversion for LDS. In conclusion, the inclusion of a new fluorescent material was compatible with the standard AR process of photovoltaic glass with no additional cost.

ce:section.ce:para: Photovoltaic (PV) technology converts solar energy into electricity, and has received considerable attention in the last two decades because of incoming grid parity. Several materials and methods have been utilized to fabricate various SCs, such as crystalline silicon (c-Si) SCs [1] and thin film SCs. The relevant materials include CuInGaSe [2], perovskite [3], and so on. Among SCs, c-Si SCs dominates the PV market owing to their high stability, continuous cost reduction, mature supply chain, and production methods. Thus far, little improvement has been achieved in the power-conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of the commercially available c-Si SCs, although slight efficiency enhancement would be highly valuable. Meanwhile, the solar spectrum is wide, ranging from 0.3 to 2.5 μm, but many PV materials have limited light absorption ranges. To achieve high utilization of the solar spectrum, fluorescence down- and up-conversion procedures have been proposed and developed [4]. These have been utilized to increase the light absorption, consequently, the cell efficiency.

When the photon energy is higher than the bandgap energy of crystalline Si (approximately 1.12 eV), particularly in the ultraviolet (UV) region, except in the case of carrier generation, excessive photon energy is wasted in the form of heating. Such heating enhances the SC working temperature and deteriorates the performance. Meanwhile, UV light is mostly absorbed on the SC surface, increasing the carrier recombination probability due to the interfacial defects. Last but not least, to ensure durability and outdoor usability, SCs should be encapsulated by glass and ethylene vinyl acetate (EVA) as solar panels. However, EVA is slowly degraded by solar irradiation and glass has poor UV transmission [5]. Overall, photons in the UV range of 300–400 nm are not utilized effectively, and approximately 6%–8% of the UV portion of solar irradiation is excluded from actual PV module absorption. We define this exclusion as the optical loss of the cell to module (CTM). Luminescence down-shifting (LDS) of UV light is a promising optical means of converting the unavailable UV radiation into longer-wavelength light to improve the photocurrent of the PV device. Various LDS materials have been investigated, such as II-VI semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [6], Si QDs [7], and organic dyes [8]. Increased cell efficiency has been demonstrated by enhancing incident light. For instance, Lee applied graphene QDs to the non-textured surfaces of Si SCs and achieved efficiency improvement of 2.94% relatively [9]. Thus far, the effects of these materials have been demonstrated conceptually at the SC level. However, these down-shifting materials have their own limitations in the actual application of solar panels. Distributing UV-excited luminescent materials inside a transparent matrix is the key to actual utilization; however, to achieve long stability and better dispersion, most down-shifting materials are embedded inside EVA layers [10], which are located under glass that blocks some of the UV light. Therefore, placing down-shifting materials on the top of solar panels is a wise approach to achieve maximal utilization of UV radiation.

Compared with the above mentioned down-shifting materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have several advantages for PV applications, such as a low-cost fabrication process, long-term chemical and photo-stability, wide designed emission wavelengths, and easy chemical linking with matrices via hydrogen bonds [11]. PL with high quantum efficiency is usually obtained from a dispersed CQD aqueous matrix; thus, most research on fluorescent CQDs has been focused on bio-applications [12] or blue emissions for white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) [13,14]. In fact, it is difficult to utilize CQDs as a luminescence source when incorporated with a solid matrix, as hydrophilic CQDs can aggregate together under dry conditions, seriously quench luminescence. To avoid aggregation inside the solid matrix, surface functionalization of CQDs is needed. The process is quite complicated, including generation, purification/separation, functionalization of CQDs, and incorporation inside the solid matrix. A simple and high-yield process is desirable for achieving mass application of CQDs in solid matrices. Correct matrix selection is critical, and various matrix materials have been used, such as organic polymer chain [15], starch, and inorganic matrices-including silica [16], BaSO4 [17], and NaCl [18]. These CQD-containing materials are almost shown the application for white LED (WLED, but a few have been discussed for PV application thus far, which requires both high transparency and high outdoor stability.

Among above solid matrices, silica is the most stable one and can retain its own properties. Thus far, the most successful application of porous silica film fabricated by the sol-gel process is the typical anti-reflection (AR) coating used on the glass at the top of a solar panel [19]. Porous silica is usually produced by hydrolysis, condensation, and the subsequent dry process involving tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) as source [20], ethanol as a solvent, and HCl or NH4OH as a catalyst [21]. To achieve additional AR effects, researchers have investigated various surface silica morphologies, such as biomimetic moth-eye structures with designed regular patterns [22] and multi-layer structures with gradually varying refractive indices [23]. Optimizing the porous top silica layer morphology improves the AR ability, and additional functions have been implemented for actual outside application, such as coating AR SiO2 with TiO2 to achieve abrasion-resistance and anti-dust properties [24], including organosilicone into TEOS to form a hybrid AR SiO2 layer for anti-scratch characteristics. Meanwhile, introducing a new luminescent function into transparent glass is rarely discussed, as luminescent material may reduce the transmission of the glass itself. But the dimensions of CQDs are much less than the wavelengths of the entire solar emission spectra, and the light scattering effect is negligible for resulting from introducing a certain amount of CQDs into solar panel sealing materials. Recently, Wang fabricated full-color fluorescent CQDs using o-phenylenediamine (oPD) as a carbon skeleton and ethanol as a solvent [25]. These ethanol-based CQDs showed wide emission wavelength ranges under UV excitation, and the measured quantum yields (QYs) were comparable to most reported to date [26].

Considering the same solvent of ethanol, here we report on an simple and effective means of anchoring synthetic luminescent CQDs with porous silica frames using chemical bonds in the TEOS sol-gel process. Further, utilizing the dual functions of porous silica, AR and LDS, the glass transparency is retained. An encapsulated solar panel using this silica coating was tested, and electrical improvement was demonstrated. To the best of our knowledge, no previous report has been published on the use of dual-functional silica coating on top of a solar panel to achieve electrical gain. This strategy could compensate for the optical loss of SCs to modules and lead to the application of LDS materials in actual solar panels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethanol-based luminescent CQD and CQD/silica sol preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Here all reagents were of analytical grade, purchased from Aladdin, and used as received. Tempered low-Fe solar glass was obtained from Dongguan CSG solar glass Co., Ltd. Fig. 1 (a) and Fig. 1(b) give the schematic synthetic and anchoring processes, respectively [27]. We firstly synthesized a luminescent CQD solution by the solvothermal method as follows: 0.2 g oPD and 1.6 ml acetic acid were mixed into 20 ml ethanol solution until complete dissolved; then, the solution was transferred into a 25 ml Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. When the reaction temperature had decreased to room temperature, the finished black-brown solution was purified by dialysis in 10 times its volume of ethanol solvent. Afterwards, modified the sol-gel method was used to prepare the CQD-linked silica sol. Firstly, 1 ml deionized water, 25 ml dialytic ethanol from above, 25 ml pure ethanol, and 0.2 ml 37% HCl were mixed under magnetic stirring at 50 °C for 10 min. Next, 4 ml TEOS was dropped into this solution while mixing slowly. Further mixing was performed under magnetic stirring at 35 °C for 2 h. Finally, this solution was stored at 30 °C for aging as silica sol for several days until it was ready for use. The solution was called the “sample with prime AR coating+5% CQD anchoring”. During the aging step, CQDs could join the condensation of Si(OH)4 oligomerization and anchor to Si–O networks, if using dialytic ethanol. Three additional sol solutions were prepared using the same recipe and process: one with pure ethanol without CQD as the “sample with prime AR coating”, one with 3/4 dialytic ethanol as the “sample with prime AR coating+7.5% CQD anchoring”, and last one with full dialytic ethanol as the “sample with prime AR coating+10% CQD anchoring”.

To evaluate the anchoring effect of the CQDs on the glass and atomic composition, XPS of the CQD‐anchored glass was performed, and the results are presented in Fig. 2 . To retain the transparency of the porous silica film after CQD anchoring, UV–visible (Vis) spectra were measured for four coated glasses using a UV-1601PC UV–Vis spectrophotometer, as shown in Fig. 3 , and the CQD concentrations of these samples ranged from zero to high. Photoluminescence (PL) excitation by 360 nm UV radiation was performed to study the fluorescence behaviors of the CQDs under different conditions. The absorption profile of 0.1% CQD containing ethanol solution and PL spectra were recorded using UV-1601PC UV–Vis spectrophotometer and F-4500 fluorescence, respectively, are presented in Fig. 4 . For PL, one sample was hydrolyzed TEOS sol with CQDs, and the other was glass with porous solidified silica containing CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mini solar panel fabrication with dual-functional silica coating
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate a porous dual-functional silica layer on a solar panel, based on the transmission measurements, we selected the 10 ml aged sol called the prime AR coating + low CQD anchoring sample to coat onto sliced glass with standard dimensions of 6 inch. After removing the ethanol by drying at room temperature, the coated glass was placed into a muffle furnace and sintered at 300 °C for 10 min to form a stable, porous silica layer. To evaluate the PV performance, we encapsulated one metal wrap-through (MWT) SC (Nanjing Sunport Power Corp. site) as a mini panel. We utilized the standard laminating process (vacuum hot pressing at 160 °C for 20 min) to fabricate the mini solar panel and laminated another sister MWT SC with almost the same electronic parameters using a standard process as a reference sample. This reference sample was AR-coated by silica sol using pure ethanol.

For the PV performance, I–V measurements of the samples were obtained by the solar simulator IPCE 7-SCSpec Newport 94023A under AM1.5 standard conditions. The external quantum efficiency (EQE) was measured using an Oriel Quantum Efficiency Measurement kit with a spot size of approximately 2 mm2.

The blue emission generated by the defect states of CQDs is popular, and the selection of carbon sources is wide. CQDs with down-shifts to yellow or orange wavelengths are preferred for PV applications because of band gap matching, with both crystalline Si and perovskite SC. According to Ref. [18], ethanol-based solvothermal formed CQDs can emit full-band color by using only one precursor oPD, but with various acid engineering approaches. Tuning is performed using the electron-withdrawing/donating groups of the acid on the CQD surface, where the PL wavelengths and CQD sizes are positively related to the amount of electron-withdrawing groups. This method could yield CQDs with large Stokes shifts, and we selected acetic acid to synthesize CQD with yellow/orange emission, as shown in Fig. 1(a).

To prove the above assumption and evaluate the anchoring effect, XPS survey spectra of the CQD/glass sample surface were obtained, as shown in Fig. 2(a). To solidify the conclusion, XPS measurement for prime glass was also done, as shown in Fig. 2(b). As we can see that C peak, the main trace of CQD couldn't find in Fig. 2(b). These results revealed strong C 1s and O 1s peaks, which originated from generated CQDs; however, the weak peaks confirmed the existence of covalent bonding of the doped N to the CQD frame. The deconvoluted C1s spectra in Fig. 2(c) demonstrated the existence of CC (284.6 eV), C–N (285.6 eV), C–O (285.1eV), and CO (288.4 eV). The O1s spectra in Fig. 2(d) displayed the presence of CO (531.3 eV) and C–OH/C–O–C (532.4 eV). The deconvoluted N1s spectra in Fig. 2(e) revealed the peaks of C–N–C (399.5 eV) and N–H (399.7 eV). In the Si 2p spectra in Fig. 2(f), the peaks with binding energies of 102.3 eV and 103.1 eV were assigned to Si–C and Si–O bonds, respectively. The presence of Si–C and Si–O bonds indicated the chemical linking of CQD with silica matrix. Overall, we believe that N-doped CQD distributed evenly inside the silica matrix in our case.

This anchoring occurred upon TEOS condensation and depended on the temperature, concentration, pH of the TEOS solution, and time. Thus, if these CQDs are incorporated into the TEOS solution in advance, they will anchor to Si–O chains anyway. TEOS hydrolysis and condensation are well-studied processes: hydroxy from Si(OH)4 will link together to form a Si–O network as SiO2, and synthetic CQDs could link to Si–O chains via their own hydroxy as anchoring sites during the TEOS aging period, as shown in Fig. 1(b). Therefore, we believe that this distribution of fluorescent CQDs is a mild process and synchronous with Si(OH)4 condensation. After the anchoring has been completed, CQDs will not be aggregated during the subsequent SiO2 solidification; thus, the luminescent property could be retained. In fact, linking CQDs with silica matrices is a frequent research topic. For example, Wang directly synthesized CQD-grafted silica nanoparticles from rice husks, retaining the luminescent property [28], and Liu produced multicolor luminescent CQDs from polymer/silica nanocomposites, using surfactant-modified silica spheres as carriers [29]. Most researchers have taken the advantage of stability and popularity of the silica matrix. Although there has been little solar-related research until now, Huang studied the interaction between aminosilane-functionalized, N-doped CQDs and the silica matrix for using as an efficient laminated luminescent solar concentrator [30]. The long-life emission from CQD-SiO2 was also deeply investigated and could be explained by multiple effects from the CQD-silica complex, including covalent bonds and the trapping effect stabilizing the triplet state between the CDs and inorganic SiO2 [31]. Therefore, we believe that embedding CQDs into silica matrices without sacrificing luminescence and transparency should be a valuable research topic for PV applications.

For the PV application of the synthesized CQDs, glasses with anchoring CQDs with different concentrations were prepared. The UV–Vis transmission spectra of these CQD/glass samples in Fig. 3 showed some interesting facts. First, all samples have rather high transmissions, whose average was more than 90%. The zoomed-in spectra in the inset indicated that the CQDs absorbed 300–400 nm UV radiation (approximately 2% transmission reduction), which is not a property of glass. However, CQD loading might affect the overall transmission of glass, which is key factor in PV performance. In Fig. 3, the sample with prime AR coating+5% CQD anchoring has approximately the same transmission as the sample with only prime AR coating, but 7.5% and 10%, these high loading CQD anchoring samples exhibited reduced transmission to a certain extent. In general, a balance should be achieved between the CQD anchoring concentration and glass transmission for further application. This characteristic also enabled the glass transmission to be tuned to match solar spectra with deviations higher than standard one, such as that occurring in a high altitude zone having strong UV radiation. Finally the samples with prime AR coating+5% CQD anchoring had been selected in the rest charaterizations, to compare with AR coating samples.

Fig. 4 presents two spectra, one is absorption profile of 0.1% CQD ethanol solution, the other is the fluorescence spectra of the CQD-ethanol sol and CQD/glass. The solution for absorptoion spectra was made by standard dialysis, free drying and resolve processes to remove impurities. Absorption profile appears a typical feature of CQD having strong absorption aournd UVA region (320–400 nm). In fact for solar AM1.5 spectra, most UV part is UVA region, and less than 320 nm is almost negligible. Overall, CQD is a best choice as LDS for solar utilization, because its absorption range has wonderful overlap with UV part of AM1.5 spectra. In contrast to the commonly reported pH-sensitive PL of CQDs, the PL of these CQDs was pH-stable, which could be a reason for the high luminescence retention of the CQDs-TEOS sol. Both samples had yellow luminescences centered around 550 nm with 360 nm excitation, due to the high UV absorption at 300–400 nm. Acid engineering and N doping were major reasons that the conjugated structure of CQDs experiences a large Stokes shift (UV excitation - > yellow emission) and that the original structure would be retained upon solid anchoring of the CQDs into a porous silica matrix. Meanwhile, solid CQD/glass had another standard emission peak at approximately 450 nm. This blue emission is a typical light generation for defect rich CQDs [32]. For our sample, this intrinsic defect emission was likely caused by sintering at 300 °C for 10 min to form solid porous silica on glass. This defect emission would not influence the entire PL spectrum.

For the PV performance, Fig. 5 shows averaged illuminated I–V measurements of two mini MWT solar panels with error bars, and Table 1 lists the PV test parameters. When the AR and CQD-anchored dual-functional layer was used, the performance of mini solar panel was noticeably enhanced: Isc increased from 9.99 A in the case of only AR coating to 10.08 A, and the total efficiency exhibits an absolutely increase of approximately 0.18%, from 20.76% to 20.94%, whereas the variations in Voc and FF were negligible. Therefore, localization of the luminescent CQDs at the topmost part of the solar panel was crucial to compensate for the optical loss of the CTM.

To understand the underlying cause of the Isc improvement, EQE measurements of two mini MWT solar panels were obtained, as presented in Fig. 6 . The AR + CQD anchoring film exhibited higher EQEs in the 300–400 nm UV range than its twin panel with only AR film, as previously described. However, in the rest of the spectrum, the role of the CQDs was not prominent. At long wavelengths, the EQE was even slightly lower than that of the panel with only AR film, probably due to the surface scattering by the aggregated CQDs inside porous silica. Anchoring effect could fix some CQDs at silica sites evenly, but not all. The aggregation of CQDs still happened more less inside matrix, and could affect the transmission as well as EQE of CQD containing samples. But gain at lower wavelength was enough to compensate. Overall, our method enabled facile scalable synthesis of anchoring porous silica, was highly compatible with solar panel encapsulation, and obviously enhanced the PV performance. Undoubtedly the stability of this dual functional silica coating was still need to investigated.

Utilizing the covalent bonds between CQDs and a silica frame, we designed a simple and compatible method of producing a porous and transparent but luminescent top anti-reflective silica layer on glass for solar panel lamination. Optical and structural characterizations were subsequently performed to prove the anchoring effects of the CQDs to the porous silica matrix. The anti-reflective silica layer formed with down-shifting CQDs located on the uppermost of the solar panel also provided UV absorption without any attenuation, and subsequent fluorescence could help to enhance the efficiency of the crystalline Si solar panel. Specifically, Isc and the absolute PCE of the solar panel coated with the CQD-anchored porous silica layer were improved by 90 mA and 0.18%, respectively, at the optimal CQD anchoring concentration inside the top glass, compared with those of a twin panel using only AR porous silica coating. We achieved optimal conversion of the UV portion of the solar spectrum, which was previously blocked by glass and EVA, providing the crystalline Si solar panel with greater power output. It is promising to apply this luminescent site anchoring method to the current anti-reflective silica layer, which could enable LDS technology to be utilized at the solar panel level.

Tiezheng Lv: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Writing, Review&Polishing, and. Yao Tang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Haonan Fan: Investigation, Formal analysis, and. Su Liu: Methodology, and. Shengqu Zeng: Funding acquisition. Wei Liu: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declared that we have no conflicts of interest to this work. We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge Nanjing Sunport Power Corp, Ltd, for solar module fabrication. This study was financially supported by the “HengCai JiaoZhi 2020 Nr.67” with contractNr.202010031490 Hengyang science and technology Bureau, by the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (Grant No. 2020JJ4261), by the Scientific Research Foundation of Hunan Provincial Education Department, China (Grant No. 20A143) and scientific research project of Hunan Provincial Department of Education (Grant No. 18B463).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning solar absorption spectra via carbon quantum dots/VAE composite layer and efficiency enhancement for crystalline Si solar module
